Which edge case doesn't `words()` handle?

```python
>>> from pydash import words
>>> def lower_case(text):
...     return ' '.join(words(text)).lower()
... 
>>> tests = (('fooBar', 'foo bar'),
... ('--foo-Bar--', 'foo bar'),
... ('*Foo*B_a*R', 'foo b a r'),
... ('*Foo10_B*Ar', 'foo 10 b ar'),
... ('/?*Foo10/;"B*Ar', 'foo 10 b ar'),
... ('/?F@O__o10456?>.B?>";Ar', 'f o o 10456 b ar'),
... ('FoO Bar', 'fo o bar'),
... ('F\n\soO Bar', 'f so o bar'),
... ('Foo1054665Bar', 'foo 1054665 bar'))
>>> for case, expected in tests:
...    lower_case(case) == expected
... 
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
True
```

The purpose of `words()` is to do all the things you mention. It may be a complicated regex, but it's what Lodash uses (NOTE: `words()` may need to be updated if Lodash has changed their regex since pydash was last updated).

Basically, any function in pydash that needs to break things up on "words" should use the `words()` function. All of the `*_case()` functions are using `words()` under the hood.

Well, not sure how this would be determined otherwise? Previously this always used DTL. We currently don't have any process-wise definition of which expression engine should be used for fields like `data_name`. In CWL, there are the `requirements` that a process can define, but we don't have that (only a language for the program, but this is something different).

One possible way would be to add something like the `requirements` from CWL, so the process definition would have to say something like this at the top level:
```yaml
requirements:
    - expression-engine:jinja
```

And then everything would use that expression engine. We could also then just have the `bash` execution engine instead of `bash-jinja` and reuse this configuration also for expressions inside the program. This would also bring our implementation closer to CWL. What do you think?

The reason that we used the `PLUGINS` module variable convention is that it turns out inspecting all the names in a module will *also* include names that the module imports. So it was possible to have the *same* plugin class be imported twice or more. Do you avoid that here? Presumably if two different plugins both import the same abstract hook, then in this case it would appear twice and be instantiated below twice, once for each module that imported it. Is that the intended behavior?

It might be better to iterate over all plugin modules and add each hook class to a set before iterating over the set to ensure that it's instantiated below only *once* even if two different modules import it.

The upshot is that the root environment's `Scripts` directory must always remain in the PATH, but it must come after the `Scripts` directory of the active environment.

This is a little bit confusing, because `GET /api/smart_proxies` only lets you search by one location or organization at a time:

    GET /api/smart_proxies
        List all smart proxies
        location_id: Scope by locations
        organization_id: Scope by organizations

However, the POST and PUT calls let you work with multiple locations or organizations. For example:

    POST /api/smart_proxies
        smart_proxy[location_ids]: REPLACE locations with given ids
        smart_proxy[organization_ids]: REPLACE organizations with given ids
        ...

The take-away is that the `SmartProxy` entity really does have one-to-many relationships with organizations and locations, and the GET call is just limited.

>>> @closeable
... class Kirby(object):

Hiiii

If this is NOT how you intend this function to work, then use a non-mutable default argument.  If a tuple can work for you then that is fine. It's really just an optimization for the code in `TaskPending` and `TaskCanceled`, so that we can request/cancel objects produced by a task if that task becomes pending or canceled locally. The alternative is to have a single `ObjectDependencyMap` and then a separate map from task ID to object IDs produced by the task. I didn't really like having to update two maps, though. Undecided on which one ends up being cleaner in the end; do you have a preference?

apply_* is a high-level interface to apply an operation onto some variables. It's ultimate goal is to provide a way to declare computational graphs like Keras and Tensorflow. That is, ultimately we can feed some variables into an apply_* function and then a new variable will be declared and returned. We are still half-way to the destination as you can see that appy_* requires users to specify output names and doesn't return output type, which is not idea. Let me summarize our status and my design goal; the only different is (1).

[Current]
1. Apply_* consumes input names and output names
2. Apply_* automatically create operator names if needed
3. Apply_* hides some details for specifying ONNX attrubutes so that supporting multiple onnx versions is easier.

[Ultimate]
1. Apply_* consumes input variables and produce output variables
2. Apply_* automatically create operator names if needed
3. Apply_* hides some details for specifying ONNX node attrubutes so that supporting multiple onnx versions is easier.
